spec_reconstruction.domain: Current U.S. federal banking board obligations for Category I global systemically important bank holding companies and their insured depository institution subsidiaries.
spec_reconstruction.unit_of_analysis: Each discrete current, in-force board-level obligation, with precise legal or supervisory source, duty, actor allocation, director-type distinction, and reform relevance.
structural_axes: entity tier: Category I GSIB BHC versus insured depository institution subsidiary
actor: full board versus board committee
committee type: audit, risk, compensation, nominating/governance, fiduciary, compliance, technology, or other designated committee
director type: inside, outside, independent, non-independent, risk-expert, interested director, and disqualified director distinctions
source tier: statute, regulation, enforceable guideline, supervisory guidance, examination manual, enforcement template, and interagency standard
issuing authority: Federal Reserve, OCC, FDIC, CFPB, FFIEC/interagency, FinCEN/OFAC where relevant

regulatory domain: safety and soundness, capital, stress testing, liquidity, recovery, resolution, operational resilience, cybersecurity, third-party risk, model risk, BSA/AML, consumer compliance, incentive compensation, audit, internal controls, fiduciary, affiliate, and insider-transaction governance
actor_classes_required: Category I GSIB BHC full board
insured depository institution subsidiary full board
source_universe: Federal Reserve regulations, SR/CA letters, guidance, and examination/supervisory materials
OCC regulations, enforceable guidelines, bulletins, Comptroller's Handbook material, and licensing guidance
FDIC regulations, guidance, examination material, and IDI resolution-planning rules
CFPB supervision and examination materials relevant to consumer compliance governance
Federal statutes underlying board-level banking obligations
coverage_areas: consumer compliance, fair lending, UDAAP, privacy, and CRA oversight
audit, internal control, financial reporting, fiduciary, insider, affiliate, and director-qualification obligations
spec_reconstruction.audience_use: Bank directors, their legal counsel, and policymakers seeking a working reform document to identify duplicative, overlapping, and burdensome board-level requirements.
spec_reconstruction.form_depth_expectations: Granular, citation-specific 40-60 page inventory with current-law status, actor mapping, committee/director distinctions, and reform-useful sludge analysis.

material_omissions: The report is not organized around Category I GSIB BHCs and their IDI subsidiaries; it broadly covers 'insured depository institutions, bank holding companies, savings associations, and large banking organizations'.
It does not provide a citation-level table of discrete current obligations; most entries are prose summaries with broad source references such as '12 CFR Part 252' or 'CFPB supervision materials'.

It lacks systematic BHC-versus-IDI subsidiary mapping and does not separate Category I GSIB-specific obligations from all-bank, national-bank, FSA, trust, public-company, and supervisory-practice material.
Director-type distinctions are scattered rather than inventoried; inside/outside and independent/non-independent distinctions are not consistently attached to obligations.
The nominating/governance committee and compensation committee are mentioned, but their obligations are thin and not treated as full actor classes.
Model risk governance is not given a standalone inventory section despite being explicitly required by the original request.
Recovery planning is treated as current through 12 CFR Part 30 Appendix E even though OCC rescinded that appendix effective May 1, 2026.
The report is about 9,000 words and reads as a 'starting document', not the requested extensive 40-60 page working legal inventory.

results.citation: 12 CFR Part 30, Appendix A; 12 CFR Part 364, Appendix A; Fed safety-and-soundness standards
results.reason: The interagency safety-and-soundness guidelines support internal controls, internal audit, credit, IRR, asset quality, and compensation standards, including audit committee/board review of internal audit systems: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/guidance/interagency-guidelines-establishing-standards-for-safety-and-soundness.htm

results.citation: 12 CFR Part 30, Appendix D
results.reason: OCC heightened standards support risk-governance framework, active board oversight, independent directors, training, and self-assessment duties: https://www.occ.gov/news-issuances/news-releases/2014/nr-occ-2014-117.html

results.citation: FDIC corporate governance materials; FDIC examination guidance
results.reason: The source reference is too generic to verify a specific current board obligation or distinguish binding law from FDIC supervisory expectations.

results.citation: 12 CFR 5.51; 12 CFR Part 303, Subpart F; Federal Reserve director/senior-officer notice requirements
results.reason: OCC and FDIC sources support prior notice requirements for changes in directors or senior executive officers in specified circumstances: https://www.occ.gov/publications-and-resources/publications/comptrollers-licensing-manual/files/licensing-booklet-changes-directors-senior-exec-officers.html

results.citation: 12 CFR Part 363; 12 CFR 363.5
results.reason: FDIC Part 363 supports annual audit/reporting duties and audit committee independence/oversight requirements for covered IDIs: https://www.fdic.gov/resources/bankers/corporate-governance-and-auditing-programs/internal-external-auditing-programs/part-363.html

results.citation: 12 CFR Part 252
results.reason: Federal Reserve Regulation YY section 252.33 supports board-level risk committee, independent chair, risk expertise, charter, reporting, and oversight requirements: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/section-25233-risk-management-and-risk-committee-requirements.htm

OCC fiduciary audit guidance supports the fiduciary audit committee composition and exclusion of officers significantly involved in fiduciary activities: https://www.occ.gov/publications-and-resources/publications/comptrollers-handbook/files/internal-external-audits/pub-ch-audits-previous.pdf

results.citation: 2023 Interagency Guidance on Third-Party Relationships: Risk Management
results.reason: The interagency guidance supports board/designated-committee oversight, reporting, risk appetite consistency, and possible approval of higher-risk or critical-activity third-party plans: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/guidance/interagency-guidance-on-third-party-relationships.htm

Federal Reserve capital-plan rule requires the BHC board or designated committee to review capital adequacy processes, remediate deficiencies, and approve the capital plan: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/section-2258-capital-planning-and-stress-capital-buffer-requirement.htm

results.citation: 12 CFR Part 252 company-run stress testing
results.reason: Federal Reserve stress-testing provisions include board or committee review/approval of stress-testing policies and consideration of results: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/section-25215-methodologies-and-practices.htm

Section 252.34 expressly assigns board responsibilities for liquidity risk tolerance and liquidity risk-management strategies, policies, and procedures: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/section-25234-liquidity-risk-management-requirements.htm

Section 252.35 supports liquidity stress-testing governance, though the report should have paired it with 252.34 for board/risk-committee duties: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/section-25235-liquidity-stress-testing-and-buffer-requirements.htm

results.citation: OCC recovery-planning standards, including 12 CFR Part 30, Appendix E
results.reason: OCC rescinded 12 CFR Part 30 Appendix E recovery-planning guidelines, effective May 1, 2026; treating them as current is stale: https://www.occ.gov/news-issuances/bulletins/2026/bulletin-2026-10.html

results.citation: Dodd-Frank Act section 165(d) resolution-planning rules
results.reason: Federal Reserve Regulation QQ section 243.4 requires covered-company resolution-plan board approval before submission: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/section-2434-resolution-plan-required.htm

results.citation: FDIC covered insured depository institution resolution-planning rule
results.reason: FDIC 12 CFR 360.10 requires CIDI board approval of full resolution submissions and minutes notation: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-12/chapter-III/subchapter-B/part-360/section-360.10

Federal Reserve BSA rule confirms written BSA compliance program approval by the board and notation in minutes; parallel OCC/FDIC rules support the same point: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/section-20863-procedures-for-monitoring-bank-secrecy-act-compliance.htm

results.citation: SAR regulations issued by OCC, Federal Reserve, FDIC, and FinCEN
results.reason: FFIEC BSA/AML manual confirms SAR regulations require board or board-committee notification, with confidentiality limits: https://bsaaml.ffiec.gov/manual/AssessingComplianceWithBSARegulatoryRequirements/04

results.citation: Sections 23A and 23B of the Federal Reserve Act; 12 CFR Part 223
results.reason: Regulation W imposes transaction restrictions and some policies/procedures requirements, but the sampled source does not support a specific board obligation to oversee policies, limits, and controls: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/regulation-w-transactions-between-member-banks-and-their-affiliates.htm

results.citation: Depository Institution Management Interlocks Act; 12 CFR Part 26 and parallel agency rules
results.reason: Management-interlocks rules prohibit covered interlocking relationships but do not, as cited, impose a board-level duty to screen, monitor, prevent, and document: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/regulations/regulation-l-management-official-interlocks.htm

results.citation: CFPB supervision materials; CFPB and prudential supervisory guidance
results.reason: CFPB CMS procedures include Board and Management Oversight and are examiner guidance; CFPB expressly says the manual is not binding legal authority: https://www.consumerfinance.gov/compliance/supervision-examinations/compliance-management-review-examination-procedures/

actor_mapping.note: Most entries include a Board actor line and some committee distinctions, but actor mapping is not granular enough for Category I GSIB BHC versus IDI subsidiary, full board versus specific required committee, or inside/outside and independent/non-independent directors.

binding_vs_guidance.note: The prose often uses calibrated terms such as 'expected' and acknowledges examination practice, but the inventory lacks a source-type column and frequently lists statutes, regulations, guidance, manuals, and enforcement practice together under 'Primary sources'.

reform_usefulness.note: The taxonomy, sludge patterns, committee-equivalence idea, and reform architecture are useful, but they need to be rebuilt on a current, citation-level actor/source map.

critical_flags.evidence: The report cites "12 CFR Part 30, Appendix E" as a current recovery-planning source.
The report repeatedly states broad duties like "Boards oversee policies, limits, controls, and compliance" without board-specific source support.
The report scopes itself to "insured depository institutions, bank holding companies, savings associations, and large banking organizations" rather than Category I GSIBs.

D1_coverage.evidence: The report covers "insured depository institutions, bank holding companies, savings associations, and large banking organizations".
D1_coverage.rationale: It covers many domains at a high level, but materially evades the required Category I GSIB BHC/IDI scope, lacks current-law actor/source granularity, and omits a standalone model-risk inventory. F6 caps the effective score.

D2_integrity.evidence: Many entries list only broad sources such as "12 CFR Part 252".
D2_integrity.rationale: Most sampled legal sources support some portion of the described duty, but citations are often broad, vague, or non-pinpoint; one central recovery-planning citation is stale, two sampled claims are not board-specific, and one central source reference was unverifiable.

D3_accuracy.evidence: The report cites "OCC recovery-planning standards, including 12 CFR Part 30, Appendix E".
D3_accuracy.rationale: The report is often legally plausible and sometimes carefully calibrated, but it contains a central stale current-law item and recurring institution-level-to-board-duty conversions. Central F3/F4 caps D3 at zero.

D4_sludge_analysis.rationale: The report has strong reform architecture: recurring obligation patterns, committee overlap, board-management boundary problems, resilience-planning consolidation, and concrete levers to consolidate, delegate, clarify, or preserve obligations.

D5_structure.evidence: It says each obligation "should be coded using the following fields".
D5_structure.rationale: The document is readable and logically organized, but it is a narrative draft rather than the requested granular citation-level inventory; the useful dataset structure is proposed rather than actually implemented.

D6_calibration.evidence: The report notes: "The formal notification duty is generally operational".
D6_calibration.rationale: The report is more transparent than many outputs: it distinguishes exam practice in places, uses 'expected' for guidance-based material, and starts as a draft/working inventory. It still does not systematically classify binding versus non-binding sources.

strengths: D4: The report offers a strong policy-sludge framework, including committee-equivalence rules, reporting tiers, common board verbs, and resilience-planning consolidation.
D6: It is relatively calibrated in places, for example treating the computer-security incident rule as operational rather than inventing a direct board notice duty.

D1/D5: It covers many relevant domains, including BSA/AML, liquidity, capital, resolution, information security, third-party risk, consumer compliance, audit, fiduciary, insider, and compensation governance.
D5: Most sections use a consistent template with source, applicability, board obligation, actor, verbs, sludge concern, and potential reform.
weaknesses: D1: It materially misses the requested Category I GSIB BHC and IDI-subsidiary scope and instead surveys a broad universe of banking organizations.
D3: It treats OCC 12 CFR Part 30 Appendix E recovery planning as current even though OCC rescinded that appendix effective May 1, 2026.
D2/D3: Several broad 'board obligation' statements are not supported by the cited source as board-specific duties, especially Regulation W and management interlocks.
D5: The report is not a citation-level inventory; many sources are named only by part, manual, agency, or generic guidance category.
D1: Model risk governance, director-type distinctions, and nominating/governance committee obligations are materially thin or missing.
prioritized_remediation: Rebuild the document as a real inventory table with one row per discrete current obligation and columns for entity tier, actor, director type, source type, citation, duty, cadence, documentation, and reform lever.
Remove or update stale recovery-planning references to OCC 12 CFR Part 30 Appendix E and replace them with current resilience, liquidity, contingency-funding, and resolution-planning obligations.
Add a Category I GSIB filter and separately map BHC obligations, IDI subsidiary obligations, and obligations that apply to both levels.
Reclassify broad compliance, CRA, fair-lending, UDAAP, Reg W, and interlocks entries as institution/management obligations unless a board-specific source is identified.
Add missing actor-class coverage for model risk, compensation committee, nominating/governance committee, inside/outside directors, and independent/non-independent directors.
narrative.reliance_recommendation: Use this report as a strong reform-concept memo, not as a current legal inventory for directors or counsel without a full citation-by-citation rebuild.
